title: Layering compost in a raised bed: building fertility from below
subtitle: Hugelkultur principles, lasagne composting, and annual compost top-up ratios for productive vegetable beds
excerpt: A raised bed built with layers of compostable material below the growing surface creates a self-fertilising foundation that improves over several years. The method (sometimes called hugelkultur or lasagne composting) uses logs, cardboard, green material, and compost to build soil structure and fertility without synthetic inputs.
sourceNotes: Charles Dowding no-dig growing method documentation. RHS guide to raised bed gardening. Sepp Holzer Permaculture (original Hugelkultur principles). HDRA (now Garden Organic) vegetable growing guides. Agriculture and Horticulture Development Board vegetable growing in raised beds guidance.

content.text: Layered raised bed construction places compostable materials at different depths below the growing surface. They decompose slowly and release nutrients over time. This is slower than hot composting. The approach works well on thin, compacted, or waterlogged ground where improving the soil directly would take many seasons.

This method buries logs and branches under the bed soil. The wood rots over 3-5 years. It holds water as it breaks down. The bed stays moist in dry weather. Plant food seeps out over time. A 200-400 mm log layer beneath 300 mm of soil and compost creates a bed that stays moist in dry weather and requires less watering in its first 3-4 years. The best logs are alder, birch, poplar, hazel, or apple. Avoid walnut (allelopathic) and cedar (slow to break down).
Lasagne composting lays organic layers on grass or weeds, without digging:
Mow or cut the existing vegetation down to ground level.
Lay overlapping cardboard sheets (remove tape and staples) to smother the existing weeds. Wet the cardboard thoroughly.
Add a 100-150 mm layer of high-carbon material: straw, wood chip, or shredded cardboard.
Add a 50-100 mm layer of high-nitrogen material: grass clippings, kitchen compost, comfrey, nettles.
Repeat layers 3 and 4 to build up 300-500 mm total depth. Finish with 100-150 mm of mature compost as the growing layer.

For an established raised bed in continuous vegetable production, apply 100-150 mm of mature compost as a surface mulch in autumn or early spring without digging. Earthworms pull it down. A 1.2 m x 2.4 m bed needs around 35-45 litres of compost per annual top-up (roughly half a standard compost bag or one barrow load from your own pile). More intensive cropping (3+ crops per year from the same bed) may need 150-200 mm per year.
